title: Should You Accept a Counteroffer After You Resign?
summary: Usually not. A counteroffer fixes your paycheck, not the reasons you wanted to leave, which is why so many people quit again within months.
content:
Should you accept a counteroffer after you resign?

Most of the time, you shouldn't accept a counteroffer after you resign. It buys you a raise, but it almost never fixes the things that pushed you to start interviewing in the first place: the manager you don't trust, the work that bores you, the ceiling you've already hit. You walk back to the same desk with a bigger number and the same problem.

There are real exceptions, and we'll get to them. But treat a counteroffer as a flag, not a gift. If your company only discovers your worth the week you try to leave, that tells you something about how they valued you the other fifty-one weeks.

Why does more money rarely fix the problem?

Pay is usually the symptom, not the disease. Think about what actually made you update your résumé. For most people it isn't the salary alone; it's a stack of smaller things the salary was supposed to make up for. Long hours. A boss who takes the credit. A role that stopped teaching you anything two years ago. An extra ₹15,000 a month feels good for about three pay cycles, and then the original frustration is sitting right where you left it.

Companies understand this, which is why a counteroffer is often a stalling tactic. Replacing you is expensive and slow, so buying ninety days of calm while they quietly plan around you can be the cheaper move. You get the raise; they get time.

Counteroffer vs. clean exit: which one pays off?

Run the two paths side by side. Accept the counteroffer and you get an immediate bump, zero onboarding, and the comfort of the known; the hidden cost is that your loyalty is now in question, you've shown your hand, and you're often first on the list when budgets tighten. Take the new job and you usually get a larger jump in base pay (external moves tend to beat internal raises by a wide margin), a fresh start, and new skills, set against the risk that the grass isn't actually greener and the first ninety days are stressful.

The cleanest tiebreaker is the reason you were leaving in the first place. If it was purely money and everything else is genuinely good, staying for the right number can be rational. If money was really a stand-in for "I'm unhappy here," the new job wins almost every time, even at the same salary.

When does saying yes actually make sense?

A few situations flip the math. If the offer comes with a concrete, written change such as a new manager, a promotion with a real title, or a move to the team you actually wanted, and not just cash, that's a genuine renegotiation rather than a bribe. Get it in writing, with a date attached.

Timing can tip it too. If you're partway through vesting, a few months from a bonus, or staring at a shaky job market where the new offer feels risky, staying for a defined stretch while you keep your options open is a defensible call. Just be honest that you're staying for a reason with an expiry date, not because the relationship healed.

What your manager is really thinking

Picture it from the other side of the table. Your resignation just handed your boss a problem they didn't budget for: a gap to cover and a rehire to run, plus a few months of someone ramping up. A counteroffer is frequently the fastest, cheapest patch for that, not a sudden reassessment of your brilliance.

Some managers do fight to keep good people, and you'll know them by how they treated you before this week. The rest are managing a deadline. Once you've signalled that you have one foot out the door, that trust is hard to rebuild, and plenty of people who stay find themselves quietly passed over at the next review anyway.

How do you say no without burning the bridge?

Keep it warm and final. Thank them sincerely, and make it clear the decision is already made: "This wasn't an easy call, and I've really appreciated working with you, but I've thought it through and I'm moving on." Skip the long justification and the list of grievances. You may cross paths with these people again, so leave the door unlocked even as you walk through it.

Resist the urge to haggle over the counteroffer for sport. Once you've decided to go, dragging it out only sours the goodbye and tempts you into a stay you'll regret by autumn.

The short version

Say no to most counteroffers. The money is real, but it rarely touches the reason you wanted out, and accepting it often marks you as a flight risk for the rest of your time there. Take one seriously only when it includes a written, structural change such as a new role or a new manager, rather than a bigger number on its own. Otherwise, thank them and decline gracefully.
